How can a system so dehumanising for everyone involved, be a solution?During the recent “discourse” over justice and social media trails, for reasons that I have been trying to explain so far, I have absolutely lost faith that I can avail of any form of justice through this tool. Social media shaming is a horrifying thing, which I don’t think I or the person in question deserves. It would be a permanent scar because remember, nothing disappears off the internet, the screenshots and accounts will keep circulating and it would end up being a traumatizing experience for both of us, without a single push towards self-improvement or actualization. I’d, of course, be slut-shamed by some, my narrative twisted and needlessly politicized and weaponized by others when all I wanted is for both of us to be better people, to create a healthy environment for discussion and understanding. While I believe that the actions of the person in question were truly wrong, it is still a huge leap from criticizing the person’s action to criticizing the person themselves, a leap I’m not willing to make. Whatever accusation I throw, irrespective of the severity of it, will be oversimplified and misappropriated by labeling them as “abuser”, “predator” and whatnot, with absolutely no acceptance for his side of the story. What if I have been emotionally abusive? That’s not far off. What if I have been manipulative? Even if you extend me immense trust that I am not, I can assure you no amount of honesty and humility will make me admit to my own mistakes as openly when I ax him for his. And why will I be worthy of this trust in the first place? What if I’m someone you don’t know? Or someone you don’t like? By now, this is a real possibility. How can I appeal for justice based on the trust I have “earned” through my social capital, how is that just for the person I’m accusing? How is that just for you, as a third party, to be pushed into accepting my account as gospel, and shun someone you barely know into a dark hole of rejection? THE PROBLEM IS BIGGER THAN THATWhy I felt the need to say all of this is that the dumpster fire that Facebook is right now, what we are doing is actively escalating conflict instead of promoting any way to come to a mutual understanding and fight together for justice. We are creating respective echo chambers for feminists and men’s rights activists, and everyone who does not agree to our exact brand of feminism, we push them outside to fend for themselves. Someone who has his life thrown into disarray for a crime he didn’t even understand that he committed since we gave him no space for introspection and discussion, which echo chamber do you think he’ll find himself in? Is that what we want? I’m not misdirecting my anger, I’m not calling any feminism “imperfect” or “pseudo”. I’m trying to appeal to you, to reconsider and reevaluate what exactly we are fighting for, and why we have shifted. Needless to say, call-out culture is effective when it comes to men who are so powerful and harmful, that letting them speak is an assault to the idea of democracy itself. But in a temporary, reactionary attitude to reprimand these people, we’re potentially ruining lives which could’ve been bettered, and more importantly derailing a movement which is crucial and can effectively bring exactly criminals like these to justice. Movements are meant to be moulded through criticism and introspection. But it seems impossible now to challenge narratives and expect any respectful engagement, and that’s precisely what I want to change. For example, I find the phrase “Believe women” very misleading. At least how it’s used conventionally. In a country like India, where women are judged, slut-shamed, raped, blamed for it, and then shoved in a corner of disbelief, this phrase is very crucial. What this implies is that an overwhelming majority of violence against women is real, and a huge percentage of it is disbelieved simply because they are not men. Believe women means don’t disbelieve someone because they are women, it does NOT mean believe everyone who is a woman.We have to understand, no matter how overwhelmingly true the accounts may be, “believe women” when set as a norm, will be very easy to abuse. You can tell me I’m wrong, correct me, and engage, I’m willing to learn, but how many of are up for that effort? There’s a particular amount of comfort in just labeling me an “enabler”, a “sympathizer”, etc, and just shut out everything I tried to challenge. The difficult part is self-introspection, to identify where we are going wrong, to question everything, EVERYTHING.As you had promised you have passed me the mic. How does it matter if no one is willing to listen?Important note - I have attempted to be as inclusive in my language as possible, if you notice any exclusion, it’s not deliberate, please correct me.
